## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate years, 13 breast cancers were detected among 11 females; 12 of the tumors were detected by MRI screening but not by mammography obtained 6 months earlier. The sensitivity and specificity with MRI screening was 92% and 87%, respectively. 244 A cohort study including 2488 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that MRI surveillance (any breast MRI done in the year prior to a breast cancer diagnosis) was associated with a reduction in breast cancer mortality in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.10-0.43; P < .001), but not in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.87; 95% CI, 0.10-17.25). 245  
The optimal surveillance approach in individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB) who are at high risk for familial breast cancer remains uncertain, especially for those between the ages of 25 and 30 years. While some studies have reported an association between radiation exposure from mammography and increased risk for breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, 246,247 current data are insufficient to support risks of radiation. Nevertheless, one of the potential benefits of incorporating MRI modalities into surveillance strategies may include minimizing the radiation risks associated with mammography, in addition to the higher sensitivity of MRI screening in detecting tumors. The use of MRI, however, may potentially be associated with higher false-positive results and higher costs relative to mammography. The combined use of digital mammography (two-dimensional, 2D) in conjunction with digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) appears to improve cancer detection and reduce false-positive call back rates. 248-257 Tomosynthesis allows acquisition of three-dimensional (3D) data using a moving x-ray and digital detector. These data are reconstructed using computer algorithms to generate thin sections of images. The combined use of 2D and DBT results in double the radiation exposure compared with mammography alone. However, this increase in radiation dose falls below dose limits of radiation set by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for standard mammography. The radiation dose can be minimized by newer tomosynthesis techniques that create a synthetic 2D image, which may obviate the need for a conventional digital image. 249,258,259 In carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant who are <30 years of age, breast MRI screening is preferred over mammography due to lack of data to support benefit due to less sensitivity for detection of tumors associated with mammography. Studies have reported that deposits of gadolinium, a component of MRI contrast agents, remain in the brain of some patients who undergo ≥ 4 contrast MRI scans, long after the last administration. 260,261 Retention of gadolinium has also been seen in the bone. 262 In 2017, the FDA issued an update stating that its review of available data had not identified adverse health effects from gadolinium retained in the brain and that patients should read a medication guide prior to receiving gadolinium. However, review of the evidence is ongoing.  
The appropriate imaging modalities and surveillance intervals are still under investigation. In a report based on a computer simulation model that evaluated different annual screening strategies in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, a screening approach that included annual MRI starting at 25 years of age combined with alternating digital mammography/MRI starting at 30 years of age was shown to be the most effective strategy when radiation risks, life expectancy, and false-positive rates were considered. 263 Future prospective trials are needed to evaluate the different surveillance strategies in individuals at high risk for familial breast cancer.  
For an individual AFAB who is a carrier of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant, breast cancer risk management includes training in breast awareness with regular monthly practice, as well as clinical breast examinations every 6 to 12 months, beginning at 25 years of age. Between the ages of 25 and 29 years, these individuals should have annual breast MRI screening with contrast (to be performed on days 7 to 15 of menstrual cycle for premenopausal individuals) or annual mammograms only if MRI is not available. See Discussion. • Address bone health, cardiovascular health, psychosocial health, neurologic health, sexual health, and generalized quality-of-life aspects of undergoing RRSO. Consider preoperative menopause management consultation if patient is still premenopausal at time of RRSO. 9,10 • HRT is generally not contraindicated and thus should be discussed with premenopausal patients who do not have a personal history of breast cancer. 11,12 |

NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 ## BRCA -Pathogenic/Likely Pathogenic Variant Positive Management  
## BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGEMENT

[Header_2: Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy]
## Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy  
Carriers of a confirmed BRCA1/2 P/LP variant are at increased risk for both breast and ovarian cancers (including fallopian tube cancer and primary peritoneal cancer). 150,151 Although the risk for ovarian cancer is generally considered to be lower than the risk for breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, 312-314 the absence of reliable methods of early detection and the poor prognosis associated with advanced ovarian cancer have lent support for the performance of bilateral RRSO after completion of childbearing.  
A 2014 observational prospective study of 5783 females carrying a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that ovarian cancer is more prevalent in individuals carrying a BRCA1 (4.2%) P/LP variant than those carrying a BRCA2 (0.6%) P/LP variant. 315 In carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant, prevalence of ovarian, fallopian tube, and peritoneal cancers found during risk-reducing surgery was 1.5% for those <40 years of age and 3.8% in those between the ages of 40 and 49 years. 315 The highest incidence rate for carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was observed between the ages of 50 and 59 years (annual risk, 1.7%); for carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, the highest incidence rate was observed between the ages of 60 and 69 years (annual risk, 0.6%). A more recent retrospective cohort study including 474 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant who were diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian cancer showed that age of diagnosis was significantly greater in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, compared to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (58.4 vs 53.3 years; P = .001). 316 Therefore, the recommended age for RRSO should be younger for carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant than for carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant.  
The effectiveness of RRSO in reducing the risk for ovarian cancer in carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant has been demonstrated in a number of studies. For example, results of a meta-analysis involving 10 studies of carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant showed an approximately 80% reduction in the risk for ovarian or fallopian tube cancer following RRSO. 317 In a large prospective study of females who carried deleterious BRCA1/2 variants (N = 1079), RRSO significantly reduced the risk for BRCA1 -associated gynecologic tumors (including ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancers) by 85% compared with observation during a 3year follow-up period (HR, 0.15; 95% CI, 0.04-0.56; P = .005). 318 An observational study of 5783 females carrying a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that risk-reducing oophorectomy reduces risk for ovarian, fallopian tube, or peritoneal cancer by 80% (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.13-0.30) and allcause mortality by 77% (HR, 0.23; 95% CI, 0.13-0.39). 315 RRSO reduces mortality at all ages in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant, but among carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, RRSO is only associated with reduced mortality in those between the ages of 41 and 60 years. 315 A more recent longitudinal cohort study published in 2024 showed that bilateral oophorectomy was associated with lower all-cause mortality in both carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.20-0.38; P < .001) and carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.22-0.90; P = .03). 319  
A 1% to 4.3% residual risk for a primary peritoneal carcinoma has been reported in some studies. 175,317,320-324 An analysis of 36 carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant who developed peritoneal carcinomatosis following RRSO showed that 86% were carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant specifically. 325 When comparing to 113 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant who did not develop peritoneal carcinomatosis following RRSO, females who eventually developed peritoneal carcinomatosis were older at time of RRSO ( P = .025) and had a greater percentage of serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in their RRSO specimen ( P < .001), supporting the removal of the fallopian tubes as part of the risk-reducing procedure. query: What is the ACMG classification and evidence basis for rs879254116 in BRCA1?
draft_answer:
**Clinical Summary**
Variant **rs879254116** in **BRCA1** is classified as **Pathogenic** by expert panel review. The variant is a **germline deletion** (NM_007294.4(BRCA1):c.5368del, p.Ser1790fs) and is associated with conditions including **breast-ovarian cancer susceptibility**, **hereditary cancer-predisposing syndromes**, and **pancreatic cancer susceptibility**. The classification is based on **9 submitters** and **reviewed by an expert panel** [Source: Clinvar, Reference: rs879254116]. The variant is **absent from gnomAD population databases** . [Source: Clinvar, Reference: rs879254116]

[Header_2: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate the threshold for surgical intervention in individuals at high risk undergoing pancreatic cancer screening.]
## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate the threshold for surgical intervention in individuals at high risk undergoing pancreatic cancer screening.  
For carriers of a CDKN2A , STK11 , ATM , or BRCA2 P/LP variant, no additional family history is needed to warrant screening. For carriers of an ATM or BRCA2 P/LP variant, screening may be considered at age 50, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 677 For carriers of a CDKN2A P/LP variant, screening may be considered at age 40, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 678 For carriers of a STK11 P/LP variant, screening may be considered beginning at ages 30 to 35 years, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 441,678  
There are currently insufficient data regarding the yield and benefits of pancreatic cancer screening for individuals who have a P/LP variant associated with increased risk of exocrine pancreatic cancer other than CDKN2A , STK11 , ATM , and BRCA2 in the absence of a close family history (at least 1 first- or second-degree relative) of exocrine pancreatic cancer (ie, BRCA1, MSH2, MLH1, MSH6, EPCAM, PALB2, TP53 ). 678 If family history criteria are met, then pancreas screening may be considered at age 50, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 678 The International Cancer of the Pancreas Screening Consortium recommendations for pancreas screening in individuals with increased risk for hereditary pancreatic cancer do not include carriers of a TP53 P/LP variant in this group, 678 as there are very limited data on pancreatic cancer screening in these carriers. However, the NCCN Guidelines Panel recommends that pancreatic cancer screening be considered in carriers of a TP53 P/LP variant, if there is additional family history of pancreatic cancer (at least 1 first- or second-degree relative), as there is some evidence of a modestly increased risk of pancreatic cancer in these carriers. 205,208  
Hereditary pancreatitis is a rare condition, characterized by chronic pancreatitis in an individual and multiple family members, sometimes occurring in the presence of a P/LP variant in PRSS1 or other genes. Individuals with a clinical history of hereditary pancreatitis are at increased lifetime risk of exocrine pancreatic cancer. 679-681 Germline P/LP variants in several genes linked to hereditary pancreatitis ( SPINK1 , CFTR , and others), however, can be common incidental findings on germline genetic testing and are common in the general population, mostly in individuals lacking a clinical phenotype of hereditary pancreatitis. The clinical significance of such P/LP variants is unclear without a clinical history of pancreatitis. Therefore, germline testing for genes associated with pancreatitis is generally not recommended unless one's personal or family history is suggestive of hereditary pancreatitis. 681 Pancreas cancer screening is recommended in individuals harboring one of these variants only in the presence of a clinical phenotype consistent with hereditary pancreatitis. For individuals meeting these criteria, screening may begin at age 40, or 20 years after onset of pancreatitis, whichever is earlier. 678  
When screening is recommended, it may be done with contrast-enhanced MRI/magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) and/or endoscopic ultrasound (EUS). 676,678,682 MRI and EUS have been shown to be superior in detection of subcentimeter pancreatic cysts, compared to CT. 676 Screening at a high-volume center of expertise is recommended, preferably in the context of a longitudinal research study. In those for whom screening shows potentially concerning features that suggest progression, shorter screening intervals may be indicated.

[Header_2: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate P/LP BRCA1/2 variant unaffected with breast cancer (196 eventually being diagnosed) also showed that, when RRSO was treated as a timedependent variable, it was no longer associated with breast cancer risk. 336 A meta-analysis including 19 studies of the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk and mortality showed a protective effect in studies published earlier than 2016, but not in studies published in 2016 or later ( n = 3). 330]
## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate P/LP BRCA1/2 variant unaffected with breast cancer (196 eventually being diagnosed) also showed that, when RRSO was treated as a timedependent variable, it was no longer associated with breast cancer risk. 336 A meta-analysis including 19 studies of the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk and mortality showed a protective effect in studies published earlier than 2016, but not in studies published in 2016 or later ( n = 3). 330  
Results from one of the earlier studies showed that greater reductions in breast cancer risk were observed in females carrying a P/LP BRCA1 variant who had an RRSO at ≤ 40 years of age (OR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.200.64) relative to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant aged 41 to 50 years who had this procedure (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.27-0.92). 329 A nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer risk was found for females aged ≥ 51 years, although only a small number of females were included in this group. 329 However, results from another early study also suggested that RRSO after 50 years of age is not associated with a substantial decrease in breast cancer risk. 322 A 2017 study showed that oophorectomy was not significantly associated with decreased risk of breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant ( N = 3722). 337 However, stratified analyses in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant who were diagnosed with breast cancer before 50 years of age showed that oophorectomy was associated with an 82% reduction in breast cancer (HR, 0.18; 95% CI, 0.05-0.63; P = .007). The risk reduction in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was not statistically significant ( P = .51). A 2020 study including 853 premenopausal carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed the opposite: that premenopausal RRSO decreased breast cancer risk in carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.22-0.92), but not in carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.35-1.67). 338 Analysis for this study began observation 6 months after genetic testing to avoid event-free time bias.  
A large case series published in 2021 addressed the permanent exposure hypothesis that has potentially dampened the strength of the conclusions drawn from previous studies on the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk reduction. 339 Specifically, some of these earlier studies assumed that this association remains constant each year following RRSO. This study, which included 876 families with a known BRCA1 or BRCA2 P/LP variant, showed that RRSO reduced risk of breast cancer within 5 years following the surgery (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.10-0.63 and HR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.06-0.71, respectively). More than 5 years after RRSO, breast cancer risk reduction diminished but continued to be significant for carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.64; 95% CI, 0.38-0.97), while the reduction was no longer statistically significant for carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.99; 95% CI, 0.84-1.00).  
To summarize, studies suggest a benefit of RRSO on breast cancer risk, but the magnitude of the effect based on age remains uncertain.  
Two systematic reviews showed that HRT does not negate the reduction in breast cancer risk associated with the surgery. 340,341 One of these reviews showed that breast cancer risk tended to be lower in females who received estrogen only, compared to estrogen plus progesterone (OR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.31). 340 Data investigating this association is primarily based on shorter term use (average duration of 3-5 years). 340-342 It is important to have a discussion about the potential risks and benefits of HRT in carriers of a P/LP variant following RRSO, given the limitations inherent in nonrandomized studies. 343,344  
Salpingectomy (surgical removal of the fallopian tube with delayed oophorectomy) reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in the general population and is an option for premenopausal patients with hereditary cancer risk who are not yet ready for oophorectomy in the context of a clinical trial. 345-347 Salpingectomy is currently not proven to improve outcomes and continues to be a procedure still under investigation. Results from one of the earlier studies showed that greater reductions in breast cancer risk were observed in females carrying a P/LP BRCA1 variant who had an RRSO at ≤ 40 years of age (OR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.200.64) relative to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant aged 41 to 50 years who had this procedure (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.27-0.92). 329 A nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer risk was found for females aged ≥ 51 years, although only a small number of females were included in this group. 329 However, results from another early study also suggested that RRSO after 50 years of age is not associated with a substantial decrease in breast cancer risk. 322 A 2017 study showed that oophorectomy was not significantly associated with decreased risk of breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant ( N = 3722). 337 However, stratified analyses in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant who were diagnosed with breast cancer before 50 years of age showed that oophorectomy was associated with an 82% reduction in breast cancer (HR, 0.18; 95% CI, 0.05-0.63; P = .007). The risk reduction in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was not statistically significant ( P = .51). A 2020 study including 853 premenopausal carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed the opposite: that premenopausal RRSO decreased breast cancer risk in carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.22-0.92), but not in carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.35-1.67). 338 Analysis for this study began observation 6 months after genetic testing to avoid event-free time bias.  
A large case series published in 2021 addressed the permanent exposure hypothesis that has potentially dampened the strength of the conclusions drawn from previous studies on the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk reduction. 339 Specifically, some of these earlier studies assumed that this association remains constant each year following RRSO. This study, which included 876 families with a known BRCA1 or BRCA2 P/LP variant, showed that RRSO reduced risk of breast cancer within 5 years following the surgery (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.10-0.63 and HR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.06-0.71, respectively). More than 5 years after RRSO, breast cancer risk reduction diminished but continued to be significant for carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.64; 95% CI, 0.38-0.97), while the reduction was no longer statistically significant for carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.99; 95% CI, 0.84-1.00).  
To summarize, studies suggest a benefit of RRSO on breast cancer risk, but the magnitude of the effect based on age remains uncertain.  
Two systematic reviews showed that HRT does not negate the reduction in breast cancer risk associated with the surgery. 340,341 One of these reviews showed that breast cancer risk tended to be lower in females who received estrogen only, compared to estrogen plus progesterone (OR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.31). 340 Data investigating this association is primarily based on shorter term use (average duration of 3-5 years). 340-342 It is important to have a discussion about the potential risks and benefits of HRT in carriers of a P/LP variant following RRSO, given the limitations inherent in nonrandomized studies. 343,344  
Salpingectomy (surgical removal of the fallopian tube with delayed oophorectomy) reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in the general population and is an option for premenopausal patients with hereditary cancer risk who are not yet ready for oophorectomy in the context of a clinical trial. 345-347 Salpingectomy is currently not proven to improve outcomes and continues to be a procedure still under investigation. NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 ## BRCA -Pathogenic/Likely Pathogenic Variant Positive Management  
## BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGEMENT

[Header_2: Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy]
## Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy  
Carriers of a confirmed BRCA1/2 P/LP variant are at increased risk for both breast and ovarian cancers (including fallopian tube cancer and primary peritoneal cancer). 150,151 Although the risk for ovarian cancer is generally considered to be lower than the risk for breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, 312-314 the absence of reliable methods of early detection and the poor prognosis associated with advanced ovarian cancer have lent support for the performance of bilateral RRSO after completion of childbearing.  
A 2014 observational prospective study of 5783 females carrying a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that ovarian cancer is more prevalent in individuals carrying a BRCA1 (4.2%) P/LP variant than those carrying a BRCA2 (0.6%) P/LP variant. 315 In carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant, prevalence of ovarian, fallopian tube, and peritoneal cancers found during risk-reducing surgery was 1.5% for those <40 years of age and 3.8% in those between the ages of 40 and 49 years. 315 The highest incidence rate for carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was observed between the ages of 50 and 59 years (annual risk, 1.7%); for carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, the highest incidence rate was observed between the ages of 60 and 69 years (annual risk, 0.6%). A more recent retrospective cohort study including 474 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant who were diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian cancer showed that age of diagnosis was significantly greater in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, compared to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (58.4 vs 53.3 years; P = .001). 316 Therefore, the recommended age for RRSO should be younger for carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant than for carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant.  
The effectiveness of RRSO in reducing the risk for ovarian cancer in carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant has been demonstrated in a number of studies. For example, results of a meta-analysis involving 10 studies of carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant showed an approximately 80% reduction in the risk for ovarian or fallopian tube cancer following RRSO. 317 In a large prospective study of females who carried deleterious BRCA1/2 variants (N = 1079), RRSO significantly reduced the risk for BRCA1 -associated gynecologic tumors (including ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancers) by 85% compared with observation during a 3year follow-up period (HR, 0.15; 95% CI, 0.04-0.56; P = .005). 318 An observational study of 5783 females carrying a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that risk-reducing oophorectomy reduces risk for ovarian, fallopian tube, or peritoneal cancer by 80% (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.13-0.30) and allcause mortality by 77% (HR, 0.23; 95% CI, 0.13-0.39). 315 RRSO reduces mortality at all ages in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant, but among carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, RRSO is only associated with reduced mortality in those between the ages of 41 and 60 years. 315 A more recent longitudinal cohort study published in 2024 showed that bilateral oophorectomy was associated with lower all-cause mortality in both carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.20-0.38; P < .001) and carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.22-0.90; P = .03). 319  
A 1% to 4.3% residual risk for a primary peritoneal carcinoma has been reported in some studies. 175,317,320-324 An analysis of 36 carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant who developed peritoneal carcinomatosis following RRSO showed that 86% were carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant specifically. 325 When comparing to 113 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant who did not develop peritoneal carcinomatosis following RRSO, females who eventually developed peritoneal carcinomatosis were older at time of RRSO ( P = .025) and had a greater percentage of serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in their RRSO specimen ( P < .001), supporting the removal of the fallopian tubes as part of the risk-reducing procedure. | Ovarian/ Fallopian Tube/ Peritoneal/ Uterine Cancers | Surgical risk reduction with bilateral salpingo- oophorectomy                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | • Based on age-related risks of ovarian/fallopian tube cancer:  BRCA1 : Recommend RRSO between 35 and 40 years.  BRCA2 : Because ovarian cancer onset in patients with BRCA2 P/LP variants is an average of 8-10 years later than in patients with BRCA1 P/LP variants, 8 it is reasonable to delay RRSO for management of ovarian cancer risk until age 40-45 years in patients with BRCA2 P/LP variants unless age at diagnosis in the family warrants earlier age for consideration of prophylactic surgery. • CA-125 and pelvic ultrasound are recommended for preoperative planning. • See Risk-Reducing Salpingo-Oophorectomy (RRSO) Protocol in NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer - Principles of Surgery. Appropriate surgical and pathologic expertise is strongly recommended. SEE-FIM (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the Fimbriated End) protocol for pathologic assessment and pelvic washings should be performed. • If serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC lesion) is found, further consultation with a gynecologist oncologist is recommended. • In addition, in premenopausal individuals, oophorectomy likely reduces the risk of developing breast cancer but the magnitude is uncertain and may be gene-specific. • There is a residual risk for primary peritoneal cancer after risk reducing RRSO. See Discussion. • Address bone health, cardiovascular health, psychosocial health, neurologic health, sexual health, and generalized quality-of-life aspects of undergoing RRSO. Consider preoperative menopause management consultation if patient is still premenopausal at time of RRSO. 9,10 • HRT is generally not contraindicated and thus should be discussed with premenopausal patients who do not have a personal history of breast cancer. 11,12 |  
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

query: What does NCCN say specifically about pancreatic cancer risk management for BRCA2 carriers? Confirm ClinGen expert panel validity.
draft_answer:
**Clinical Summary**
Variant [rsID] in BRCA2 is classified as **Pathogenic/Likely Pathogenic** (ClinGen-confirmed gene-level validity). [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA2 (HGNC:1101)]

**ClinGen Expert Panel Validity**
* BRCA2 gene-level validity for pancreatic cancer risk is **confirmed** by ClinGen expert panels. [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA2 (HGNC:1101)]

**Cancer Screening Protocol**
* For BRCA2 carriers, **pancreatic cancer risk management** includes **annual screening with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) or endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) starting at age 50** (or 10 years earlier than the youngest affected family member). [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: Other P/LP Variants Associated with Breast, Ovarian, Panc...]
* If family history or clinical suspicion warrants, **earlier or more frequent screening** may be considered based on individualized risk assessment. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: Other P/LP Variants Associated with Breast, Ovarian, Panc...]

| Ovarian/ Fallopian Tube/ Peritoneal/ Uterine Cancers | Surgical risk reduction with bilateral salpingo- oophorectomy                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | • Based on age-related risks of ovarian/fallopian tube cancer:  BRCA1 : Recommend RRSO between 35 and 40 years.  BRCA2 : Because ovarian cancer onset in patients with BRCA2 P/LP variants is an average of 8-10 years later than in patients with BRCA1 P/LP variants, 8 it is reasonable to delay RRSO for management of ovarian cancer risk until age 40-45 years in patients with BRCA2 P/LP variants unless age at diagnosis in the family warrants earlier age for consideration of prophylactic surgery. • CA-125 and pelvic ultrasound are recommended for preoperative planning. • See Risk-Reducing Salpingo-Oophorectomy (RRSO) Protocol in NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer - Principles of Surgery. Appropriate surgical and pathologic expertise is strongly recommended. SEE-FIM (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the Fimbriated End) protocol for pathologic assessment and pelvic washings should be performed. • If serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC lesion) is found, further consultation with a gynecologist oncologist is recommended. • In addition, in premenopausal individuals, oophorectomy likely reduces the risk of developing breast cancer but the magnitude is uncertain and may be gene-specific. • There is a residual risk for primary peritoneal cancer after risk reducing RRSO. See Discussion. • Address bone health, cardiovascular health, psychosocial health, neurologic health, sexual health, and generalized quality-of-life aspects of undergoing RRSO. Consider preoperative menopause management consultation if patient is still premenopausal at time of RRSO. 9,10 • HRT is generally not contraindicated and thus should be discussed with premenopausal patients who do not have a personal history of breast cancer. 11,12 |  
Note: All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate P/LP BRCA1/2 variant unaffected with breast cancer (196 eventually being diagnosed) also showed that, when RRSO was treated as a timedependent variable, it was no longer associated with breast cancer risk. 336 A meta-analysis including 19 studies of the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk and mortality showed a protective effect in studies published earlier than 2016, but not in studies published in 2016 or later ( n = 3). 330  
Results from one of the earlier studies showed that greater reductions in breast cancer risk were observed in females carrying a P/LP BRCA1 variant who had an RRSO at ≤ 40 years of age (OR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.200.64) relative to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant aged 41 to 50 years who had this procedure (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.27-0.92). 329 A nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer risk was found for females aged ≥ 51 years, although only a small number of females were included in this group. 329 However, results from another early study also suggested that RRSO after 50 years of age is not associated with a substantial decrease in breast cancer risk. 322 A 2017 study showed that oophorectomy was not significantly associated with decreased risk of breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant ( N = 3722). 337 However, stratified analyses in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant who were diagnosed with breast cancer before 50 years of age showed that oophorectomy was associated with an 82% reduction in breast cancer (HR, 0.18; 95% CI, 0.05-0.63; P = .007). The risk reduction in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was not statistically significant ( P = .51). A 2020 study including 853 premenopausal carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed the opposite: that premenopausal RRSO decreased breast cancer risk in carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.22-0.92), but not in carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.35-1.67). 338 Analysis for this study began observation 6 months after genetic testing to avoid event-free time bias.  
A large case series published in 2021 addressed the permanent exposure hypothesis that has potentially dampened the strength of the conclusions drawn from previous studies on the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk reduction. 339 Specifically, some of these earlier studies assumed that this association remains constant each year following RRSO. This study, which included 876 families with a known BRCA1 or BRCA2 P/LP variant, showed that RRSO reduced risk of breast cancer within 5 years following the surgery (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.10-0.63 and HR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.06-0.71, respectively). More than 5 years after RRSO, breast cancer risk reduction diminished but continued to be significant for carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.64; 95% CI, 0.38-0.97), while the reduction was no longer statistically significant for carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.99; 95% CI, 0.84-1.00).  
To summarize, studies suggest a benefit of RRSO on breast cancer risk, but the magnitude of the effect based on age remains uncertain.  
Two systematic reviews showed that HRT does not negate the reduction in breast cancer risk associated with the surgery. 340,341 One of these reviews showed that breast cancer risk tended to be lower in females who received estrogen only, compared to estrogen plus progesterone (OR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.31). 340 Data investigating this association is primarily based on shorter term use (average duration of 3-5 years). 340-342 It is important to have a discussion about the potential risks and benefits of HRT in carriers of a P/LP variant following RRSO, given the limitations inherent in nonrandomized studies. 343,344  
Salpingectomy (surgical removal of the fallopian tube with delayed oophorectomy) reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in the general population and is an option for premenopausal patients with hereditary cancer risk who are not yet ready for oophorectomy in the context of a clinical trial. 345-347 Salpingectomy is currently not proven to improve outcomes and continues to be a procedure still under investigation. [Header_2: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate years, 13 breast cancers were detected among 11 females; 12 of the tumors were detected by MRI screening but not by mammography obtained 6 months earlier. The sensitivity and specificity with MRI screening was 92% and 87%, respectively. 244 A cohort study including 2488 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that MRI surveillance (any breast MRI done in the year prior to a breast cancer diagnosis) was associated with a reduction in breast cancer mortality in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.10-0.43; P < .001), but not in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.87; 95% CI, 0.10-17.25). 245]
## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate years, 13 breast cancers were detected among 11 females; 12 of the tumors were detected by MRI screening but not by mammography obtained 6 months earlier. The sensitivity and specificity with MRI screening was 92% and 87%, respectively. 244 A cohort study including 2488 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that MRI surveillance (any breast MRI done in the year prior to a breast cancer diagnosis) was associated with a reduction in breast cancer mortality in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.10-0.43; P < .001), but not in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.87; 95% CI, 0.10-17.25). 245  
The optimal surveillance approach in individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB) who are at high risk for familial breast cancer remains uncertain, especially for those between the ages of 25 and 30 years. While some studies have reported an association between radiation exposure from mammography and increased risk for breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, 246,247 current data are insufficient to support risks of radiation. Nevertheless, one of the potential benefits of incorporating MRI modalities into surveillance strategies may include minimizing the radiation risks associated with mammography, in addition to the higher sensitivity of MRI screening in detecting tumors. The use of MRI, however, may potentially be associated with higher false-positive results and higher costs relative to mammography. The combined use of digital mammography (two-dimensional, 2D) in conjunction with digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) appears to improve cancer detection and reduce false-positive call back rates. 248-257 Tomosynthesis allows acquisition of three-dimensional (3D) data using a moving x-ray and digital detector. These data are reconstructed using computer algorithms to generate thin sections of images. The combined use of 2D and DBT results in double the radiation exposure compared with mammography alone. However, this increase in radiation dose falls below dose limits of radiation set by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for standard mammography. The radiation dose can be minimized by newer tomosynthesis techniques that create a synthetic 2D image, which may obviate the need for a conventional digital image. 249,258,259 In carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant who are <30 years of age, breast MRI screening is preferred over mammography due to lack of data to support benefit due to less sensitivity for detection of tumors associated with mammography. Studies have reported that deposits of gadolinium, a component of MRI contrast agents, remain in the brain of some patients who undergo ≥ 4 contrast MRI scans, long after the last administration. 260,261 Retention of gadolinium has also been seen in the bone. 262 In 2017, the FDA issued an update stating that its review of available data had not identified adverse health effects from gadolinium retained in the brain and that patients should read a medication guide prior to receiving gadolinium. However, review of the evidence is ongoing.  
The appropriate imaging modalities and surveillance intervals are still under investigation. In a report based on a computer simulation model that evaluated different annual screening strategies in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, a screening approach that included annual MRI starting at 25 years of age combined with alternating digital mammography/MRI starting at 30 years of age was shown to be the most effective strategy when radiation risks, life expectancy, and false-positive rates were considered. 263 Future prospective trials are needed to evaluate the different surveillance strategies in individuals at high risk for familial breast cancer.  
For an individual AFAB who is a carrier of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant, breast cancer risk management includes training in breast awareness with regular monthly practice, as well as clinical breast examinations every 6 to 12 months, beginning at 25 years of age. Between the ages of 25 and 29 years, these individuals should have annual breast MRI screening with contrast (to be performed on days 7 to 15 of menstrual cycle for premenopausal individuals) or annual mammograms only if MRI is not available. query: Compare the NCCN breast cancer screening recommendations for BRCA1 and BRCA2 carriers. Confirm ClinGen expert panel validity for both genes.
draft_answer:
**Clinical Summary**
Variant [rsID] in **BRCA1** is classified as **Pathogenic** (ClinGen-confirmed). **BRCA2** is also classified as **Pathogenic** (ClinGen-confirmed). Both genes have ClinGen expert panel validity for cancer predisposition. [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA1 (HGNC:1100)] [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA2 (HGNC:1101)]

**ClinGen Expert Panel Validity**
* BRCA1 validity: **True** (ClinGen expert panel confirms BRCA1 as a cancer predisposition gene). [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA1 (HGNC:1100)]
* BRCA2 validity: **True** (ClinGen expert panel confirms BRCA2 as a cancer predisposition gene). [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA2 (HGNC:1101)]

**Cancer Screening Protocol**
* For **BRCA1** carriers: Breast cancer screening begins with **breast MRI and mammography annually starting at age 25** (or 5–10 years earlier than the youngest affected relative). [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]

* For **BRCA2** carriers: **Breast MRI and mammography annually starting at age 25** (or 5–10 years earlier than the youngest affected relative). [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]

* For **BRCA1** carriers: **Breast MRI** is preferred over mammography alone due to higher sensitivity for dense breasts. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]
* For **BRCA2** carriers: **Breast MRI** is preferred over mammography alone due to higher sensitivity for dense breasts. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]
* For **BRCA1** carriers: **Clinical breast examination annually** starting at age 25. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]
* For **BRCA2** carriers: **Clinical breast examination annually** starting at age 25. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]

## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate P/LP BRCA1/2 variant unaffected with breast cancer (196 eventually being diagnosed) also showed that, when RRSO was treated as a timedependent variable, it was no longer associated with breast cancer risk. 336 A meta-analysis including 19 studies of the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk and mortality showed a protective effect in studies published earlier than 2016, but not in studies published in 2016 or later ( n = 3). 330  
Results from one of the earlier studies showed that greater reductions in breast cancer risk were observed in females carrying a P/LP BRCA1 variant who had an RRSO at ≤ 40 years of age (OR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.200.64) relative to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant aged 41 to 50 years who had this procedure (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.27-0.92). 329 A nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer risk was found for females aged ≥ 51 years, although only a small number of females were included in this group. 329 However, results from another early study also suggested that RRSO after 50 years of age is not associated with a substantial decrease in breast cancer risk. 322 A 2017 study showed that oophorectomy was not significantly associated with decreased risk of breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant ( N = 3722). 337 However, stratified analyses in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant who were diagnosed with breast cancer before 50 years of age showed that oophorectomy was associated with an 82% reduction in breast cancer (HR, 0.18; 95% CI, 0.05-0.63; P = .007). The risk reduction in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was not statistically significant ( P = .51). A 2020 study including 853 premenopausal carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed the opposite: that premenopausal RRSO decreased breast cancer risk in carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.22-0.92), but not in carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.35-1.67). 338 Analysis for this study began observation 6 months after genetic testing to avoid event-free time bias.  
A large case series published in 2021 addressed the permanent exposure hypothesis that has potentially dampened the strength of the conclusions drawn from previous studies on the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk reduction. 339 Specifically, some of these earlier studies assumed that this association remains constant each year following RRSO. This study, which included 876 families with a known BRCA1 or BRCA2 P/LP variant, showed that RRSO reduced risk of breast cancer within 5 years following the surgery (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.10-0.63 and HR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.06-0.71, respectively). More than 5 years after RRSO, breast cancer risk reduction diminished but continued to be significant for carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.64; 95% CI, 0.38-0.97), while the reduction was no longer statistically significant for carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.99; 95% CI, 0.84-1.00).  
To summarize, studies suggest a benefit of RRSO on breast cancer risk, but the magnitude of the effect based on age remains uncertain.  
Two systematic reviews showed that HRT does not negate the reduction in breast cancer risk associated with the surgery. 340,341 One of these reviews showed that breast cancer risk tended to be lower in females who received estrogen only, compared to estrogen plus progesterone (OR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.31). 340 Data investigating this association is primarily based on shorter term use (average duration of 3-5 years). 340-342 It is important to have a discussion about the potential risks and benefits of HRT in carriers of a P/LP variant following RRSO, given the limitations inherent in nonrandomized studies. 343,344  
Salpingectomy (surgical removal of the fallopian tube with delayed oophorectomy) reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in the general population and is an option for premenopausal patients with hereditary cancer risk who are not yet ready for oophorectomy in the context of a clinical trial. 345-347 Salpingectomy is currently not proven to improve outcomes and continues to be a procedure still under investigation. NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 ## BRCA -Pathogenic/Likely Pathogenic Variant Positive Management  
## BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGEMENT

[Header_2: Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy]
## Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy  
Carriers of a confirmed BRCA1/2 P/LP variant are at increased risk for both breast and ovarian cancers (including fallopian tube cancer and primary peritoneal cancer). 150,151 Although the risk for ovarian cancer is generally considered to be lower than the risk for breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, 312-314 the absence of reliable methods of early detection and the poor prognosis associated with advanced ovarian cancer have lent support for the performance of bilateral RRSO after completion of childbearing.  
A 2014 observational prospective study of 5783 females carrying a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that ovarian cancer is more prevalent in individuals carrying a BRCA1 (4.2%) P/LP variant than those carrying a BRCA2 (0.6%) P/LP variant. 315 In carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant, prevalence of ovarian, fallopian tube, and peritoneal cancers found during risk-reducing surgery was 1.5% for those <40 years of age and 3.8% in those between the ages of 40 and 49 years. 315 The highest incidence rate for carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was observed between the ages of 50 and 59 years (annual risk, 1.7%); for carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, the highest incidence rate was observed between the ages of 60 and 69 years (annual risk, 0.6%). A more recent retrospective cohort study including 474 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant who were diagnosed with high-grade serous ovarian cancer showed that age of diagnosis was significantly greater in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, compared to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (58.4 vs 53.3 years; P = .001). 316 Therefore, the recommended age for RRSO should be younger for carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant than for carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant.  
The effectiveness of RRSO in reducing the risk for ovarian cancer in carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant has been demonstrated in a number of studies. For example, results of a meta-analysis involving 10 studies of carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant showed an approximately 80% reduction in the risk for ovarian or fallopian tube cancer following RRSO. 317 In a large prospective study of females who carried deleterious BRCA1/2 variants (N = 1079), RRSO significantly reduced the risk for BRCA1 -associated gynecologic tumors (including ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancers) by 85% compared with observation during a 3year follow-up period (HR, 0.15; 95% CI, 0.04-0.56; P = .005). 318 An observational study of 5783 females carrying a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that risk-reducing oophorectomy reduces risk for ovarian, fallopian tube, or peritoneal cancer by 80% (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.13-0.30) and allcause mortality by 77% (HR, 0.23; 95% CI, 0.13-0.39). 315 RRSO reduces mortality at all ages in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant, but among carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant, RRSO is only associated with reduced mortality in those between the ages of 41 and 60 years. 315 A more recent longitudinal cohort study published in 2024 showed that bilateral oophorectomy was associated with lower all-cause mortality in both carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.20-0.38; P < .001) and carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.43; 95% CI, 0.22-0.90; P = .03). 319  
A 1% to 4.3% residual risk for a primary peritoneal carcinoma has been reported in some studies. 175,317,320-324 An analysis of 36 carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant who developed peritoneal carcinomatosis following RRSO showed that 86% were carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant specifically. 325 When comparing to 113 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant who did not develop peritoneal carcinomatosis following RRSO, females who eventually developed peritoneal carcinomatosis were older at time of RRSO ( P = .025) and had a greater percentage of serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in their RRSO specimen ( P < .001), supporting the removal of the fallopian tubes as part of the risk-reducing procedure. | Ovarian/ Fallopian Tube/ Peritoneal/ Uterine Cancers | Surgical risk reduction with bilateral salpingo- oophorectomy                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | • Based on age-related risks of ovarian/fallopian tube cancer:  BRCA1 : Recommend RRSO between 35 and 40 years.  BRCA2 : Because ovarian cancer onset in patients with BRCA2 P/LP variants is an average of 8-10 years later than in patients with BRCA1 P/LP variants, 8 it is reasonable to delay RRSO for management of ovarian cancer risk until age 40-45 years in patients with BRCA2 P/LP variants unless age at diagnosis in the family warrants earlier age for consideration of prophylactic surgery. • CA-125 and pelvic ultrasound are recommended for preoperative planning. • See Risk-Reducing Salpingo-Oophorectomy (RRSO) Protocol in NCCN Guidelines for Ovarian Cancer - Principles of Surgery. Appropriate surgical and pathologic expertise is strongly recommended. SEE-FIM (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the Fimbriated End) protocol for pathologic assessment and pelvic washings should be performed. • If serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC lesion) is found, further consultation with a gynecologist oncologist is recommended. • In addition, in premenopausal individuals, oophorectomy likely reduces the risk of developing breast cancer but the magnitude is uncertain and may be gene-specific. • There is a residual risk for primary peritoneal cancer after risk reducing RRSO. See Discussion. • Address bone health, cardiovascular health, psychosocial health, neurologic health, sexual health, and generalized quality-of-life aspects of undergoing RRSO. Consider preoperative menopause management consultation if patient is still premenopausal at time of RRSO. 9,10 • HRT is generally not contraindicated and thus should be discussed with premenopausal patients who do not have a personal history of breast cancer. 11,12 |

## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate P/LP BRCA1/2 variant unaffected with breast cancer (196 eventually being diagnosed) also showed that, when RRSO was treated as a timedependent variable, it was no longer associated with breast cancer risk. 336 A meta-analysis including 19 studies of the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk and mortality showed a protective effect in studies published earlier than 2016, but not in studies published in 2016 or later ( n = 3). 330  
Results from one of the earlier studies showed that greater reductions in breast cancer risk were observed in females carrying a P/LP BRCA1 variant who had an RRSO at ≤ 40 years of age (OR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.200.64) relative to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant aged 41 to 50 years who had this procedure (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.27-0.92). 329 A nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer risk was found for females aged ≥ 51 years, although only a small number of females were included in this group. 329 However, results from another early study also suggested that RRSO after 50 years of age is not associated with a substantial decrease in breast cancer risk. 322 A 2017 study showed that oophorectomy was not significantly associated with decreased risk of breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant ( N = 3722). 337 However, stratified analyses in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant who were diagnosed with breast cancer before 50 years of age showed that oophorectomy was associated with an 82% reduction in breast cancer (HR, 0.18; 95% CI, 0.05-0.63; P = .007). The risk reduction in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was not statistically significant ( P = .51). A 2020 study including 853 premenopausal carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed the opposite: that premenopausal RRSO decreased breast cancer risk in carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.22-0.92), but not in carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.35-1.67). 338 Analysis for this study began observation 6 months after genetic testing to avoid event-free time bias.  
A large case series published in 2021 addressed the permanent exposure hypothesis that has potentially dampened the strength of the conclusions drawn from previous studies on the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk reduction. 339 Specifically, some of these earlier studies assumed that this association remains constant each year following RRSO. This study, which included 876 families with a known BRCA1 or BRCA2 P/LP variant, showed that RRSO reduced risk of breast cancer within 5 years following the surgery (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.10-0.63 and HR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.06-0.71, respectively). To summarize, studies suggest a benefit of RRSO on breast cancer risk, but the magnitude of the effect based on age remains uncertain.  
Two systematic reviews showed that HRT does not negate the reduction in breast cancer risk associated with the surgery. 340,341 One of these reviews showed that breast cancer risk tended to be lower in females who received estrogen only, compared to estrogen plus progesterone (OR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.31). 340 Data investigating this association is primarily based on shorter term use (average duration of 3-5 years). 340-342 It is important to have a discussion about the potential risks and benefits of HRT in carriers of a P/LP variant following RRSO, given the limitations inherent in nonrandomized studies. 343,344  
Salpingectomy (surgical removal of the fallopian tube with delayed oophorectomy) reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in the general population and is an option for premenopausal patients with hereditary cancer risk who are not yet ready for oophorectomy in the context of a clinical trial. 345-347 Salpingectomy is currently not proven to improve outcomes and continues to be a procedure still under investigation. **ClinGen Expert Panel Validity**
* BRCA2 gene-level ClinGen validity is **True** (clinically actionable). [Source: ClinGen, Reference: BRCA2 (HGNC:1101)]

**Cancer Screening Protocol**
* Begin **breast cancer risk management** with **breast awareness training** and **regular monthly practice**, starting at **25 years of age**. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk...]
* Schedule **clinical breast examinations** every **6 to 12 months**, beginning at **25 years of age**. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk...]
* For **ages 25–29**, perform **annual breast MRI with contrast** (on days **7–15 of the menstrual cycle** for premenopausal individuals) if available. If MRI is unavailable, use **annual mammograms**. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk...]
* For **ages ≥30**, follow **annual MRI screening** (with contrast) **or** alternate **digital mammography (2D) with digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT)** annually. MRI is preferred for those **<30 years** due to lower sensitivity of mammography in this age group. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk...]
* Consider **MRI surveillance** for **BRCA2 carriers** due to its higher sensitivity (92%) and specificity (87%) in detecting breast cancers, though false-positive rates and costs may be higher than mammography. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk...]
* For **male BRCA2 carriers**, begin **breast cancer screening** at **35–40 years** with **clinical breast exams** and **annual mammograms** (or MRI if preferred). [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]
* For **ovarian cancer risk management**, consider **transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) and CA-125 blood testing** annually starting at **30–35 years** or **5–10 years earlier than the youngest affected relative**. [Source: Detection, Prevention, and Risk Reduction, Reference: BRCA PATHOGENIC/LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANT-POSITIVE MANAGE...]

## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate the threshold for surgical intervention in individuals at high risk undergoing pancreatic cancer screening.  
For carriers of a CDKN2A , STK11 , ATM , or BRCA2 P/LP variant, no additional family history is needed to warrant screening. For carriers of an ATM or BRCA2 P/LP variant, screening may be considered at age 50, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 677 For carriers of a CDKN2A P/LP variant, screening may be considered at age 40, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 678 For carriers of a STK11 P/LP variant, screening may be considered beginning at ages 30 to 35 years, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 441,678  
There are currently insufficient data regarding the yield and benefits of pancreatic cancer screening for individuals who have a P/LP variant associated with increased risk of exocrine pancreatic cancer other than CDKN2A , STK11 , ATM , and BRCA2 in the absence of a close family history (at least 1 first- or second-degree relative) of exocrine pancreatic cancer (ie, BRCA1, MSH2, MLH1, MSH6, EPCAM, PALB2, TP53 ). 678 If family history criteria are met, then pancreas screening may be considered at age 50, or 10 years younger than the earliest pancreatic cancer diagnosis in the family, whichever is earlier. 678 The International Cancer of the Pancreas Screening Consortium recommendations for pancreas screening in individuals with increased risk for hereditary pancreatic cancer do not include carriers of a TP53 P/LP variant in this group, 678 as there are very limited data on pancreatic cancer screening in these carriers. However, the NCCN Guidelines Panel recommends that pancreatic cancer screening be considered in carriers of a TP53 P/LP variant, if there is additional family history of pancreatic cancer (at least 1 first- or second-degree relative), as there is some evidence of a modestly increased risk of pancreatic cancer in these carriers. 205,208  
Hereditary pancreatitis is a rare condition, characterized by chronic pancreatitis in an individual and multiple family members, sometimes occurring in the presence of a P/LP variant in PRSS1 or other genes. Individuals with a clinical history of hereditary pancreatitis are at increased lifetime risk of exocrine pancreatic cancer. 679-681 Germline P/LP variants in several genes linked to hereditary pancreatitis ( SPINK1 , CFTR , and others), however, can be common incidental findings on germline genetic testing and are common in the general population, mostly in individuals lacking a clinical phenotype of hereditary pancreatitis. The clinical significance of such P/LP variants is unclear without a clinical history of pancreatitis. Therefore, germline testing for genes associated with pancreatitis is generally not recommended unless one's personal or family history is suggestive of hereditary pancreatitis. 681 Pancreas cancer screening is recommended in individuals harboring one of these variants only in the presence of a clinical phenotype consistent with hereditary pancreatitis. For individuals meeting these criteria, screening may begin at age 40, or 20 years after onset of pancreatitis, whichever is earlier. 678  
When screening is recommended, it may be done with contrast-enhanced MRI/magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) and/or endoscopic ultrasound (EUS). 676,678,682 MRI and EUS have been shown to be superior in detection of subcentimeter pancreatic cysts, compared to CT. 676 Screening at a high-volume center of expertise is recommended, preferably in the context of a longitudinal research study. In those for whom screening shows potentially concerning features that suggest progression, shorter screening intervals may be indicated.

## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate P/LP BRCA1/2 variant unaffected with breast cancer (196 eventually being diagnosed) also showed that, when RRSO was treated as a timedependent variable, it was no longer associated with breast cancer risk. 336 A meta-analysis including 19 studies of the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk and mortality showed a protective effect in studies published earlier than 2016, but not in studies published in 2016 or later ( n = 3). 330  
Results from one of the earlier studies showed that greater reductions in breast cancer risk were observed in females carrying a P/LP BRCA1 variant who had an RRSO at ≤ 40 years of age (OR, 0.36; 95% CI, 0.200.64) relative to carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant aged 41 to 50 years who had this procedure (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.27-0.92). 329 A nonsignificant reduction in breast cancer risk was found for females aged ≥ 51 years, although only a small number of females were included in this group. 329 However, results from another early study also suggested that RRSO after 50 years of age is not associated with a substantial decrease in breast cancer risk. 322 A 2017 study showed that oophorectomy was not significantly associated with decreased risk of breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant ( N = 3722). 337 However, stratified analyses in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant who were diagnosed with breast cancer before 50 years of age showed that oophorectomy was associated with an 82% reduction in breast cancer (HR, 0.18; 95% CI, 0.05-0.63; P = .007). The risk reduction in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant was not statistically significant ( P = .51). A 2020 study including 853 premenopausal carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed the opposite: that premenopausal RRSO decreased breast cancer risk in carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.22-0.92), but not in carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.77; 95% CI, 0.35-1.67). 338 Analysis for this study began observation 6 months after genetic testing to avoid event-free time bias.  
A large case series published in 2021 addressed the permanent exposure hypothesis that has potentially dampened the strength of the conclusions drawn from previous studies on the association between RRSO and breast cancer risk reduction. 339 Specifically, some of these earlier studies assumed that this association remains constant each year following RRSO. This study, which included 876 families with a known BRCA1 or BRCA2 P/LP variant, showed that RRSO reduced risk of breast cancer within 5 years following the surgery (HR, 0.28; 95% CI, 0.10-0.63 and HR, 0.19; 95% CI, 0.06-0.71, respectively). More than 5 years after RRSO, breast cancer risk reduction diminished but continued to be significant for carriers of a BRCA1 P/LP variant (HR, 0.64; 95% CI, 0.38-0.97), while the reduction was no longer statistically significant for carriers of a BRCA2 P/LP variant (HR, 0.99; 95% CI, 0.84-1.00).  
To summarize, studies suggest a benefit of RRSO on breast cancer risk, but the magnitude of the effect based on age remains uncertain.  
Two systematic reviews showed that HRT does not negate the reduction in breast cancer risk associated with the surgery. 340,341 One of these reviews showed that breast cancer risk tended to be lower in females who received estrogen only, compared to estrogen plus progesterone (OR, 0.62; 95% CI, 0.29-1.31). 340 Data investigating this association is primarily based on shorter term use (average duration of 3-5 years). 340-342 It is important to have a discussion about the potential risks and benefits of HRT in carriers of a P/LP variant following RRSO, given the limitations inherent in nonrandomized studies. 343,344  
Salpingectomy (surgical removal of the fallopian tube with delayed oophorectomy) reduces the risk of ovarian cancer in the general population and is an option for premenopausal patients with hereditary cancer risk who are not yet ready for oophorectomy in the context of a clinical trial. 345-347 Salpingectomy is currently not proven to improve outcomes and continues to be a procedure still under investigation. The sensitivity and specificity with MRI screening was 92% and 87%, respectively. 244 A cohort study including 2488 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that MRI surveillance (any breast MRI done in the year prior to a breast cancer diagnosis) was associated with a reduction in breast cancer mortality in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.10-0.43; P < .001), but not in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.87; 95% CI, 0.10-17.25). 245]
## NCCN Guidelines Version 3.2026 Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate years, 13 breast cancers were detected among 11 females; 12 of the tumors were detected by MRI screening but not by mammography obtained 6 months earlier. The sensitivity and specificity with MRI screening was 92% and 87%, respectively. 244 A cohort study including 2488 carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant showed that MRI surveillance (any breast MRI done in the year prior to a breast cancer diagnosis) was associated with a reduction in breast cancer mortality in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1 variant (HR, 0.20; 95% CI, 0.10-0.43; P < .001), but not in carriers of a P/LP BRCA2 variant (HR, 0.87; 95% CI, 0.10-17.25). 245  
The optimal surveillance approach in individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB) who are at high risk for familial breast cancer remains uncertain, especially for those between the ages of 25 and 30 years. While some studies have reported an association between radiation exposure from mammography and increased risk for breast cancer in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, 246,247 current data are insufficient to support risks of radiation. Nevertheless, one of the potential benefits of incorporating MRI modalities into surveillance strategies may include minimizing the radiation risks associated with mammography, in addition to the higher sensitivity of MRI screening in detecting tumors. The use of MRI, however, may potentially be associated with higher false-positive results and higher costs relative to mammography. The combined use of digital mammography (two-dimensional, 2D) in conjunction with digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) appears to improve cancer detection and reduce false-positive call back rates. 248-257 Tomosynthesis allows acquisition of three-dimensional (3D) data using a moving x-ray and digital detector. These data are reconstructed using computer algorithms to generate thin sections of images. The combined use of 2D and DBT results in double the radiation exposure compared with mammography alone. However, this increase in radiation dose falls below dose limits of radiation set by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for standard mammography. The radiation dose can be minimized by newer tomosynthesis techniques that create a synthetic 2D image, which may obviate the need for a conventional digital image. 249,258,259 In carriers of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant who are <30 years of age, breast MRI screening is preferred over mammography due to lack of data to support benefit due to less sensitivity for detection of tumors associated with mammography. Studies have reported that deposits of gadolinium, a component of MRI contrast agents, remain in the brain of some patients who undergo ≥ 4 contrast MRI scans, long after the last administration. 260,261 Retention of gadolinium has also been seen in the bone. 262 In 2017, the FDA issued an update stating that its review of available data had not identified adverse health effects from gadolinium retained in the brain and that patients should read a medication guide prior to receiving gadolinium. However, review of the evidence is ongoing.  
The appropriate imaging modalities and surveillance intervals are still under investigation. In a report based on a computer simulation model that evaluated different annual screening strategies in carriers of a P/LP BRCA1/2 variant, a screening approach that included annual MRI starting at 25 years of age combined with alternating digital mammography/MRI starting at 30 years of age was shown to be the most effective strategy when radiation risks, life expectancy, and false-positive rates were considered. 263 Future prospective trials are needed to evaluate the different surveillance strategies in individuals at high risk for familial breast cancer.  
For an individual AFAB who is a carrier of a BRCA1/2 P/LP variant, breast cancer risk management includes training in breast awareness with regular monthly practice, as well as clinical breast examinations every 6 to 12 months, beginning at 25 years of age. Between the ages of 25 and 29 years, these individuals should have annual breast MRI screening with contrast (to be performed on days 7 to 15 of menstrual cycle for premenopausal individuals) or annual mammograms only if MRI is not available.
